does: This paper investigates fundamental limitations of using reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) to align AI systems with human values in democratic contexts. The authors draw on social choice theory to prove two key impossibility results: first, that no unique voting protocol can universally align AI systems through democratic RLHF processes under broad assumptions, and second, that perfectly aligning AI agents with all individuals' values necessarily violates certain private ethical preferences of some users. The work establishes that universal AI alignment via RLHF is theoretically impossible and derives formal conditions under which these limitations arise. The paper grounds these abstract results in analysis of how RLHF systems currently operate in deployed large language models.
connects: This work bridges AI alignment methodology with social choice theory, extending decades of research on voting paradoxes and preference aggregation impossibility theorems. The paper directly engages with Arrow's impossibility theorem and related results about the impossibility of combining individual preferences into consistent collective decisions. It builds on recent work establishing RLHF as the dominant practical approach for aligning large language models and complements growing literature examining the societal implications of preference elicitation in AI systems. The research connects to debates about value pluralism in AI ethics and extends concerns about whose values get represented in deployed AI systems, particularly in multicultural and politically diverse contexts.
enables: This work provides theoretical grounding for developing pragmatic alternatives to democratic alignment via RLHF, such as targeted fairness constraints, preference-respecting architectures, or hybrid human-AI deliberation systems. It motivates investigation of weaker alignment objectives that sacrifice universality for other desiderata like proportional representation of values or protection of individual ethical autonomy. The impossibility results point toward policy frameworks acknowledging that perfect alignment is unattainable and focusing instead on transparency, contestability, and democratic accountability mechanisms for AI systems. Future work can explore whether domain-specific relaxations of the theoretical assumptions might enable partial alignment and how institutions should design governance structures for AI systems when universal alignment proves impossible.
